reasoning: The threshold for a YES resolution requires the 30-day event count to exceed 10 * (1 + 360-day average per 30 days). Historical ACLED data consistently shows Kenya experiencing ~20-40 'Violence against civilians' events per 30-day period. If the historical data is properly backfilled at resolution time, this 30-day average will be ~25. Thus, the target formula (10 * 26) would require over 250 events in a 1-month period—a catastrophic escalation akin to civil war, which is wholly unsupported by current conditions in Kenya. Even considering the prompt's preliminary baseline value of 1.000 (suggesting 0 events were initially queried for the preceding year), this most likely reflects an API lag or query artifact rather than a true absence of violence, as historical reports confirm ongoing incidents. When ACLED data is finalized, the baseline will likely correct to normal levels. Even in the bizarre edge case where the historical year permanently remains at 0 (setting the target to >10), it is highly improbable that a system experiencing such a severe data-collection outage would spontaneously jump to correctly logging 11+ events in the immediate following month without simultaneously backfilling the historical baseline. Consequently, whether the historical baseline accurately reflects dozens of events or is artificially nullified, it remains phenomenally unlikely that the single-month counts will achieve a true 10x multiplier over the year-long stabilized metric. The probabilities are kept near the absolute minimum configuration constraint.
